text: We present a case of robotic assisted cortical sparing left adrenalectomy in a patient with von Hippel-Lindau syndrome. As you can see, it is an autosomal dominant tumor predisposition syndrome affecting multiple organs. The frequency of pheochromocytomas and paragangliomas in these patients varies between seven and twenty percent. Among patients who develop pheochromocytomas, up to forty-seven percent will develop bilateral lesions. Our patient is a twenty-one year old male patient who underwent laparoscopic right adrenalectomy approximately six years ago for pheochromocytoma. Patient has been following with endocrinologist in order to have recent elevation in urine metanephrines. MIBG scan showed uptake in the region of the left adrenal gland as shown in this MRI picture with a nodule.

Preoperative preparation involves adrenergic blockade with phenoxybenzamine and propranol. Robotic surgical technique itself involves patient positioning in the modified right lateral decubitus position with beanbag and footboard. Assistant surgeon stands on the left and the robotic cart is brought forty-five degrees off the left shoulder. Patient is under general endotracheal anesthesia and left upper quadrant varice needle is used to insufflate the abdomen. Patient is positioned airplane table to the left to place trocars and to right to perform the operation. Steep reverse trendler bunk position is used to expose the left upper quadrant better. Trocar placement is as shown in the picture.

The surgeon uses an accessory port for the assistant surgeon. During laparoscopy, adhesions between the splenic flexure and spleen are observed and dissected using a ligature device. The left kidney is visualized, and the spleen is fully mobilized. The Gerota's fascia over the kidney and adrenal gland is identified and dissected to expose the left renal vein. The left adrenal vein is also dissected.

The surgeon clips and divides the circumferentially dissected left adrenal vein. The xerotase fascia covering the adrenal gland is dissected and removed to expose the gland fully. A laparoscopic ultrasound is used to identify the pheochromocytoma nodule, which is marked with the robot. Hook electrocautery in the robot's right arm opens the adrenal gland cortex at the nodule site.

The surgeon manages expected bleeding using a gauze piece and suction irrigator while dissecting around the identified nodule. Dissection is performed outside the pseudocapsule using bipolar cautery in the robot's left arm and hook cautery in the right arm, benefiting from the robot's stability and three-dimensional visualization.
The surgeon continues dissection around the pseudocapsule of the adrenal gland, carefully removing the nodule. Bleeding is controlled using pressure and limited electrocautery.

The patient was discharged home after a twenty-three hour observation on replacement steroids. Patient was seen in clinic at two weeks with uneventful recovery. And at two months, patient is now on half-dose steroids for maintenance. Our conclusions. Cortical sparing partial adrenectomy is feasible. Robotic assistance provides enhanced three-dimensional visualization and minimal manipulation of adjacent normal adrenal tissue.
